## Running The Compiler

@docs run

---


## Types

This module aliases the main types that you might be interested in working with.
See the appropriate modules (e.g [`Ren.Data.Expression`](./Ren-Data-Expression))
for more documentation about that type and the things you might want to do with
it.

The alises are provided here as a convenience so you only need to import
`Ren.Compiler` if you don't want to do anything fancy!

@docs Module, Declaration, Expression

---


## Parsing

@docs parse, parseDeclaration, parseExpression
@docs decode, decodeDeclaration, decodeExpression

---


## Optimisation

@docs optimise, optimiseDeclaration, optimiseExpression

---


## Emitting Target Code

@docs Target, emit, emitDeclaration, emitExpression

aliases.name: Declaration
aliases.comment:
Declarations can either be functions (declared with the `fun` keyword), or
variables (declared with the `let` keyword). Additionally, declarations may be
marked as public exports with the `pub` keyword. Here are some declarations:

    fun foo = x y =>
        x + y * z
    where
        z = 0.99

    pub let bar = "Hello world!"

    let baz = fun a => a * 2

aliases.name: Module
aliases.comment:
Modules are made up of two things. First, zero or more imports (separated by
newlines), and then zero or more declarations (also separated by new lines). A
basic module might look like:

    import 'Console' as Console

    pub fun main = _ => Console.log "Hello world!"

values.name: run
values.comment:
Run the entire compilation pipeline on a single input. This will parse the
input as a `Module`, perform a number of AST optimisations and transformations,
and then emit the module as an ES6 JavaScript module.

You can recreate this function yourself from a composition of the other functions
exposed in this module:

    import Ren.Compiler
        exposing
            ( Target(..)
            , emit
            , optimise
            , parse
            )

    run =
        parse
            >> Result.map optimise
            >> Result.map (emit ES6)

aliases.name: Accessor
aliases.comment:
An `Accessor` is what we use to access fields or indecies of an object or
array. They can be fixed as in:

    foo.bar

Or they can be computed:

    foo["baz"]

In fact, array indexing is just a computed accessor with a `Number` literal as
the computed expression:

    arr[0]

aliases.name: Identifier
aliases.comment:
An `Identifier` is what we use when we need to refer to something by its name.
Lower case names are `Local` identifiers:

    foo

But we can also have scoped identifiers if we import another module and give it
a name

    Foo.bar

We also have some more interesting types of identifiers. **Operators** can be
used as identifiers!

    (+) 1 2

And so can object fields:

    .foo obj

aliases.name: Literal
aliases.comment:
We support the typical literals that you might expect. Like JavaScript we have
a single Number literal to cover both Integers and Floats, rather than making them
distinct:

    1
    0.99

There are boolean literals:

    true
    false

String literals can use either single or double quotes:

    "Hello World!"
    'Superior quotes here'

Array literals correspond directly to JavaScript arrays (unlike other languages
that may compile arrays/lists into something else):

    [ 1, 'hello', false ]

As do object literals:

    { foo: fun x => ...
    , bar: 0.123456789
    }

aliases.name: Operator
aliases.comment:
Unlike a language like Haskell or PureScript, we don't allow custom operators.
Below is a list of all the operators Ren has, and their names. Refer to the
language docs if you want to know what all of these do!

    -- Functions
    |>  Pipe   
    >>  Compose
    ;   Discard

    -- Maths
    +   Add    
    -   Sub    
    *   Mul    
    /   Div    
    ^   Pow    
    %   Mod   

    -- Comparison 
    ==  Eq     
    !=  NotEq  
    <   Lt     
    <=  Lte    
    >   Gt     
    >=  Gte   

    -- Logic 
    &   And    
    |   Or   

    -- Arrays  
    ::  Cons   
    ++  Join

aliases.name: Pattern
aliases.comment:
Patterns are used in function arguments and encompass a few different things.
Simple name bindings are patterns:

    fun foo => ...

But we can also do JS-style array and object destructuring:

    fun { foo } => ...
    fun [ bar ] => ...

And we can nest patterns inside these destructuring patterns as you might expect:

    fun { foo: { bar } } => ...
    fun [ bar, { baz } ] => ...

And finally there is the wildcard pattern when you want to *ignore* an argument
but still require it to exist:

    fun _ => ...

The wildcard pattern can be used to "comment out" arguments by prefixing the
underscore on a simple name pattern:

    fun _foo => ...

values.name: array
values.comment: Create an array literal expression from an Elm list of expressions.

values.name: boolean
values.comment: Create a boolean literal expression from an Elm Bool.

values.name: decoder
values.comment:
The decoder used in [`fromJSON`](#fromJSON). This might be handy if you have
a JSON string rather than an Elm `Value` and you want to decode it using
`Json.Decode.decodeString`.

values.name: field
values.comment: Create an `Identifier` expression from a record field name.

values.name: fromJSON
values.comment:
Parse an `Expression` from an Elm JSON `Value`. You might find this handy if
you're working with some tooling that emits a Ren AST as JSON.

values.name: fromSource
values.comment:
Parse an `Expression` from some Ren source code. It doesn't seem all that
likely you'll need to use this over `Declaration.fromSource` or `Module.fromSource`
but just in case it is, here you go.

values.name: int
values.comment:
Create a number literal expression from an Elm Int. Remember numbers in
Ren are always floating-point, so this will convert the argument to an Elm
Float first.

values.name: local
values.comment: Create an `Identifier` expression from a local name.

values.name: number
values.comment: Create a number literal expression from an Elm Float.

values.name: object
values.comment: Create an object literal expression from an Elm list of key/value piars.

values.name: operator
values.comment: Create an `Identifier` expression from an `Operator`.

values.name: operatorFromName
values.comment: Create an `Operator` from its name used the in Elm source code.

values.name: operatorFromSymbol
values.comment: Create an `Operator` from its symbollic representation in Ren code.

values.name: operatorToName
values.comment:
Convert an `Operator` to its (unqualified) name. This is the name that is
used in the Elm source code.

values.name: operatorToSymbol
values.comment: Convert an `Operator` to its symbollic representation in Ren code.

values.name: parser
values.comment:
The parse used in `fromSource`. It's unclear why you'd need this, but it's
exposed just in case you do.

values.name: scoped
values.comment: Create an `Identifier` expression from a list of namespaces and a name.

values.name: string
values.comment: Create a string literal expression from an Elm string.

unions.name: Module
cases: { imports : List.List Ren.Data.Module.Import, declarations : List.List Ren.Data.Declaration.Declaration }

aliases.name: Import
aliases.comment:
Imports can take a few different shapes.

They can be aliased:

    import 'foo/bar' as Foo.Bar

They can expose unqualified bindings:

    import 'foo/bar' exposing { baz }

Or both:

    import 'foo/bar' as Foo.Bar exposing { baz }

Or neither:

    import 'foo/bar'

values.name: exposes
values.comment: Check if a module exposes a function or variable with the given name.

values.name: import_
values.type: String.String -> List.List String.String -> List.List String.String -> Ren.Data.Module.Import

values.name: imports
values.comment: Check if a module imports a function or variable with the given name.
